title: The Cartographer's Lament: An Enumeration of Unique Inscriptions
description: Presented is a single sacred inscription S. A 'fragment' shall denote any contiguous and non-empty excerpt drawn therefrom. Two fragments are adjudged ONE AND THE SAME if, and only if, they read precisely alike — character for character — irrespective of the station within the inscription whence each was excised. Thy charge: pronounce the cardinality of the set of all mutually-distinct fragments. (The naive may enumerate; the wise shall summon an automaton of suffixes. Either way, the road is long.)

examples.explanation: From 'abab' issue ten contiguous excerpts, yet the herald tallieth only the unalike: the singletons 'a' and 'b'; the pairs 'ab' (twice begotten, but once inscribed in the ledger) and 'ba'; the triples 'aba' and 'bab'; and the inscription entire, 'abab'. Seven fragments distinct, the second 'ab' having been struck out as a mere echo.

Behold 'aaa', deceptive in its uniformity: though six excerpts may be cut from it, they collapse — by the law of identical reading — into but THREE distinct fragments: 'a', 'aa', and 'aaa'. The duplicates dissolve like morning frost.
